content:
4 min read Just now -- You open React DevTools, flip on the “Highlight updates” setting, and click a single button in your app. Suddenly, half the screen flashes. A sidebar that has nothing to do with the button. A header. A list of items that didn’t change at all. Your first thought is probably: “None of that data changed. Why did it re-render?” This is one of the most common misunderstandings in React. Most developers assume React only re-renders a component when its own data changes. In reality, React re-renders far more liberally than that, and understanding why is the difference between guessing at performance fixes and actually knowing what’s happening. Press enter or click to view image in full size What Actually Triggers a Re-render React re-renders a component for a few specific reasons: • Its own state changes (via useState or useReducer). • Its parent re-renders, regardless of whether the props passed down actually changed. • A context value it subscribes to changes (via useContext). That second point is the one that surprises people most. When a parent component re-renders, React re-renders every child in that render tree by default, even children whose props are identical to last time. Why would React do something that seems so wasteful? Because checking “did anything actually change” is itself work, and React’s whole model is built around re-rendering being cheap. Instead of trying to be clever up front, React just re-runs your component function to get a new description of the UI, then compares that description to the previous one before touching the actual DOM. That comparison step is called reconciliation. It’s how React decides what, if anything, needs to change in the real DOM. A re-render does not mean the DOM changes; it means React is checking whether the DOM needs to change. Common Misconceptions “My props didn’t change, so my component won’t re-render.” Not true by default. If the parent re-renders, the child re-renders too, even with identical props. React.memo is what adds the "skip if props are the same" check, but you have to opt in. “React compares the DOM before rendering.” It’s the other way around. React runs your component (the render), builds a new virtual representation, diffs that against the previous virtual representation, and only then touches the real DOM if something differs. The DOM comparison happens after rendering, not before. “Every re-render is a performance problem.” Re-renders are just JavaScript function calls. They’re usually fast. The expensive part is DOM mutation, not the render phase itself. Treating every re-render as a bug leads to premature optimization. Examples • Parent re-render causes child re-render function Parent() { const [count, setCount] = useState(0); return (
); } function Child() { console.log("Child rendered"); return

I don't use count at all.

; } Every click re-renders Child, even though it never reads count. That's expected default behavior, not a bug. 2. Inline objects and functions break memoization doSomething()} />Even if Child is wrapped in React.memo, this will still re-render every time, because the config object and the inline arrow function are brand-new references on every render. React.memo uses shallow comparison — it checks reference equality for objects and functions, not deep equality. 3. React.memo in action const Child = React.memo(function Child({ label }) { console.log("Child rendered"); return

{label}

; });Now Child only re-renders if label actually changes. If the parent re-renders but passes the same label, React.memo skips re-rendering Child. To pair this with stable references, use useMemo for objects and useCallback for functions: const config = useMemo(() => ({ theme: "dark" }), []); const handleClick = useCallback(() => doSomething(), []);4. Observing renders yourself A quick console.log("Rendered") at the top of a component, or the React DevTools Profiler tab, shows you exactly when and how often something re-renders — far more reliably than guessing. When Re-renders Actually Matter Rendering in React is generally inexpensive. It’s just JavaScript running your component function and building a virtual tree. The costly part — actual DOM mutation — only happens when reconciliation finds real differences. This means: • A component re-rendering does not mean the DOM updated. • Optimizing rendering before you’ve measured is usually wasted effort. • The cases where it does matter: components doing expensive computation on every render, large lists, deeply nested trees, or components wrapping heavy third-party UI like charts, editors, or maps. In those cases, React.memo, useMemo, and useCallback are useful tools, but they add their own overhead (comparison logic, extra memory), so applying them everywhere "just in case" can make things slower, not faster. Takeaways • Re-rendering is React’s default, low-cost behavior — not a signal that something is broken. • A parent re-rendering re-renders all its children by default, regardless of prop changes. • Rendering is not the same as DOM updates. React reconciles first, then mutates the DOM only where needed. • React.memo skips re-renders only when props are shallowly equal — inline objects and functions defeat it. • Don’t optimize based on render count alone; measure actual performance impact first. • Reach for React.memo, useMemo, and useCallback only for genuinely expensive components or large lists.
